claims.claim_text: The SolarWinds Orion compromise was a real software supply-chain incident relevant to trust in signed artifacts and build pipelines.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: This is a named real-world incident and CISA directly documents the SolarWinds Orion supply-chain compromise.

claims.claim_text: Log4Shell created emergency work across thousands of downstream teams.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Log4Shell <!-- incident-xref: log4shell --> showed how one vulnerable library can create emergency work across thousands of teams that never wrote a line of logging framework code.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Log4Shell <!-- incident-xref: log4shell --> showed how one vulnerable library can create emergency work across many downstream teams that never directly authored the affected dependency.
claims.rationale: The named incident is real, but the precise scope claim about 'thousands of teams' is not directly backed by the primary advisory and should be de-specified.

claims.claim_text: In the OCI model, an image manifest is content-addressed by digest and represents a specific image for a specific platform.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Establish Artifact Identity with Digests and Signatures, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The OCI image manifest specification directly describes content-addressable image manifests and their role in artifact identity.

claims.claim_text: Cosign is the most common command-line tool for signing and verifying OCI artifacts in the Sigstore ecosystem.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Establish Artifact Identity with Digests and Signatures, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Cosign is the most common command-line tool for signing and verifying OCI artifacts in the Sigstore ecosystem.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cosign is a widely used command-line tool for signing and verifying OCI artifacts in the Sigstore ecosystem.
claims.rationale: The superlative 'most common' is an uncited comparative benchmark and should be rewritten without the unsupported ranking.

claims.claim_text: Cosign signs and verifies OCI artifacts and supports Sigstore keyless signing using Fulcio and Rekor.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Establish Artifact Identity with Digests and Signatures, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: The Cosign primary project page directly describes OCI artifact signing/verification and keyless operation with Fulcio and Rekor.

claims.claim_text: Fulcio issues short-lived code-signing certificates for OIDC identities.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Establish Artifact Identity with Digests and Signatures, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: The Fulcio project README explicitly states that it issues short-lived certificates for code signing tied to OIDC identities.

claims.claim_text: Rekor records software supply-chain signing metadata in a transparency log for later verification and auditability.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Establish Artifact Identity with Digests and Signatures, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: The Rekor project README directly describes Rekor as a transparency log for software supply-chain metadata.

claims.claim_text: SPDX is an SBOM standard associated with the Linux Foundation ecosystem, and its common serializations include formats such as tag-value, RDF, and JSON.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Add SBOMs and Vulnerability Scans as Evidence, SBOM FORMATS diagram
claims.rationale: The exact serialization details are best backed by the SPDX specification site, which is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: CycloneDX is an OWASP BOM standard and commonly uses JSON and XML encodings.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Add SBOMs and Vulnerability Scans as Evidence, SBOM FORMATS diagram
claims.rationale: The CycloneDX specification repository directly states OWASP stewardship and lists the standard media types and encodings.

claims.claim_text: Syft generates SBOMs from container images, filesystems, and directories.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Add SBOMs and Vulnerability Scans as Evidence, paragraph after SBOM FORMATS diagram
claims.rationale: The Syft project README directly documents SBOM generation from container images and filesystem-style sources.

claims.claim_text: Trivy can scan images, filesystems, repositories, Kubernetes targets, and work with SBOMs.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Add SBOMs and Vulnerability Scans as Evidence, paragraph after Syft introduction
claims.rationale: The Trivy project README directly lists its supported scan targets and scanners, including SBOM-related capabilities.

claims.claim_text: Harbor is a CNCF graduated registry.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Use Registries and Admission Control as Enforcement Points, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The exact graduated-project status is documented on Harbor's official site, which is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Harbor supports policy features such as automatic scan-on-push and preventing vulnerable images from running.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Use Registries and Admission Control as Enforcement Points, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: These are real Harbor product capabilities, but the primary Harbor docs host is not presently allowlisted.

claims.claim_text: Ratify is a Kubernetes verification engine for artifact security metadata that can gate admission on policy compliance.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Use Registries and Admission Control as Enforcement Points, paragraph 6
claims.rationale: The Ratify project README directly describes Ratify as a verification engine that admits only artifacts complying with policy.

claims.claim_text: Gatekeeper provides constraint-based policy enforcement with audit and external data support.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. Use Registries and Admission Control as Enforcement Points, paragraph 6
claims.rationale: The Gatekeeper README directly lists constraints, audit functionality, and external data support.

claims.claim_text: SLSA is a software supply-chain security framework for increasing levels of integrity and trust.
claims.span_hint: section: 6. Operate the System: Exceptions, Provenance, and Incident Response, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: The SLSA framework repository directly defines SLSA as a software supply-chain security framework.

claims.claim_text: in-toto provides a framework for protecting software supply-chain integrity by verifying planned steps and authorized functionaries.
claims.span_hint: section: 6. Operate the System: Exceptions, Provenance, and Incident Response, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: The in-toto project README directly explains the framework's step verification and functionary authorization model.

further_reading.title: sigstore/cosign
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary documentation for OCI signing, verification, attestations, and keyless workflows used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: OCI Image Manifest Specification
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative source on image manifests, content addressing, and artifact identity by digest.

further_reading.title: SLSA Framework
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary framework reference for provenance-oriented supply-chain maturity and build integrity concepts.

notes: No claims were emitted for quiz prompts, exercise steps, diagrams, or code blocks unless surrounding prose made a factual product/framework claim. The shared section pool did not contain exact-match sources for the Sigstore, SPDX, Harbor, Ratify, Gatekeeper, SLSA, or in-toto claims.
